steps.m: for us (connected to سَقَيْتَ - you watered)
steps.n: Added نا which means 'us' to show who the action was done for. This word connects to سَقَيْتَ (you watered) to show that the watering was done for us.

steps.m: and when/then when (connects to previous statement about her coming)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this part with what happened before (her coming to him)

steps.m: and when/then when (connects to جَآءَهُۥ - he came to him)
steps.n: The word لَمَّا with shadda shows that one action (him coming) happened right after another (her inviting him). It connects to the next word جَآءَهُۥ (he came to him).
Added vowels and connected letters. The middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a hollow verb. This word follows لَمَّا (when) in the ayah.
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) for pronunciation, but doesn't change the meaning
Added هُ with a small و (ۥ) after it to show this is the object - the person being come to

steps.m: to tell a story in detail

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels and doubled the middle letter ص to show emphasis and completeness in telling the story

steps.m: and he told (the story of مَا سَقَيْتَ لَنَا)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this with the previous part where she came to him. The story he tells refers back to how he helped them with the water.
Added vowel marks to show the correct pronunciation of the preposition
Changed the final ى to ي with sukun to prepare for the attached pronoun

steps.m: upon him (referring to the story قَصَّ)
steps.n: Added هِ (him) at the end to show who is being referred to. This refers back to the story (قَصَّ) that was mentioned.

steps.m: to tell a story or describe events in order

steps.n: Adding vowel marks makes this a past tense verb
Added 'al' (the) at the beginning to make it 'the story' instead of just 'story', and it's the thing being told in the sentence
Added fathas to make it past tense. This shows someone did the action in the past.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is very common in hollow verbs
This is a negative particle that tells someone not to do something. Here it's telling someone not to fear, working with the word تَخَفْ that comes after it.
Connected the root letters together. The middle letter و changes to ا when used in past tense.
Adding ت at start changes it from 'he' to 'you' and makes it present tense
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final و changes to ا because it's a weak letter at the end.
The ا changes back to وْ before adding a suffix

steps.m: you escaped (after being saved from الْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّالِمِينَ - the wrongdoing people)
steps.n: Added تَ at the end to show 'you' (masculine) did this action in the past. This word connects to the next phrase 'from the wrongdoing people' to complete its meaning.
Basic preposition showing movement away from something or separation
Added fatha at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word الْقَوْمِ (the people). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'people' - the connection is that people are those who establish and maintain society

steps.m: the people (who are being fled from)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and ِ to show it follows مِنَ (from). This refers to the people Moses fled from in Egypt.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he did wrong' (past tense verb) to 'wrongdoer' (active participle/doer of action). Describes the people chasing Moses.
Forms regular masculine plural. This word describes and belongs to القَوْمِ (the people), forming the phrase 'the wrongdoing people'.
Adds 'the' (ال) to make it specific wrongdoers, and connects to 'the people' mentioned
Added short vowels (fat-ha marks) to make it mean 'he said' in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
Added ت with sukun at the end to show the subject is feminine. This word is connected to إِحْدَىٰهُمَا (one of them [feminine]) who is doing the speaking.
Root letters connected with fatha vowels (َ) to make the number one
Changed from masculine 'one' to feminine 'one' by adding ى at the end and changing the first vowel to إِ

steps.m: one of them two (f) (subject of قَالَتْ - she said)
steps.n: Added هُمَا to show it refers to two females. The ٰ (alif) extends the ى sound. This word is the subject of قَالَتْ (she said) in this ayah.
Changed from the root letters for 'father' into the simple noun meaning 'father' by adding vowels
Added ت at the end to make it feminine, like when a daughter calls her father
Added kasra at the end to show we are calling out to father, like saying 'O father!'
Added the vocative particle يا with elongation (ٰ) and a glottal stop (ٓ) to call out to someone
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added است at the beginning to show seeking or requesting the action
Changed from 'he hired' to 'you hire!' by making it a command word
Added هُ at the end to show who should be hired
Basic particle that adds emphasis to what follows
Added kasra under إ and shadda to make the emphasis stronger. This particle introduces and emphasizes the word خَيْرَ (best) that comes after it in this ayah.
Basic noun pattern showing the quality of being good
Added fatha at the end because it's the object of إِنَّ (indeed). In this ayah, it's being used to say that someone is the best person to hire.
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. In this ayah, it refers to the person who was hired.
Added kasra at the end because it's followed by a word starting with a hamza (ٱسْتَـْٔجَرْتَ). This makes it easier to pronounce.
Added basic vowel marks to connect the root letters
Form X verb showing someone seeking to do the action
The ت at the end changes it from 'he hired' to 'you hired' - speaking to someone (in this case Moses)
Root meaning 'strong' became an adjective meaning 'very strong' by adding shadda
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific strong one
Adding damma shows this describes the main person being talked about in the sentence

steps.m: to be trusted or feel secure

steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to show someone who has this quality strongly
Added ال to make it specific and ُ to show it's describing Moses. This is part of what one of Moses's future wife's says about him to her father.

steps.m: he said (introducing what he is about to say)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word introduces his direct speech that follows.
Weak letter transformation from و to ا
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. The shadda (ّ) makes it stronger.
Added ي at the end to show this is about me. This word connects to أُرِيدُ (I want) in this ayah.
The ي is written as ى with an alif (ٓ) above it when it comes at the end of a word. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic concept of wanting or seeking something
Adding the basic vowels to make it a complete past tense verb

steps.m: that, to (connects to أُرِيدُ - I want)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows what someone wants to do or what will happen. Here it connects 'I want' (أُرِيدُ) with what the speaker wants to do - give his daughter in marriage.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed to causative form (making someone do something) and first person

steps.m: I give you in marriage (object of أُرِيدُ - I want)
steps.n: Added pronoun 'you' as the person being given in marriage. This whole phrase is what is being wanted in the sentence (object of 'I want')
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together
Changed from masculine 'one' to feminine 'one' by changing the first vowel and adding a rest mark

steps.m: one of (referring to one of his two daughters)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to make it mean 'one of' instead of just 'one' - like saying 'one of the daughters' instead of just 'one daughter'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed vowels and added ت to make a new word meaning 'daughter' - this is actually a separate word, not directly derived from 'to build'
Added اِ at start to make it a special form of the word 'daughter'
Added a fatha mark at the end to show this word is being used as a possessive or object
Changed to show we're talking about two daughters
This is the basic demonstrative word used for pointing to something
The alif is written as a long vowel mark above the ه
Adding ت makes it specifically for feminine things
Adding ي shows we're talking about exactly two things

steps.m: these two (feminine) (referring to ٱبْنَتَىَّ - my two daughters)
steps.n: Adding نِ at the end shows this is being used as the object of a verb or after a preposition. Here it's pointing to 'my two daughters' mentioned in the ayah.
Basic preposition showing something is physically on top of or metaphorically based on something else
The alif at the end is written with a small mark above (ٰ) to show it's a long sound
The small mark (ٓ) shows this word connects to what comes after it in meaning. Here it connects to أَن تَأْجُرَنِى (that you work for me)
This is a connecting word that joins two ideas together. Here it connects 'I want' (أُرِيدُ) with what is wanted. It works like 'that' in English sentences like 'I want that you...'
Connected the root letters together to form the base word
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' doing the action, with appropriate vowel marks
Changed the ending to fatha because it follows أَنْ (that)
Added نِى at the end to show the action is happening to 'me'
This is a number word that directly means 'eight'
Changes from singular 'eight' to plural form meaning 'group of eight'

steps.m: eight (as a counted number in this ayah)
steps.n: Changed to the form used when counting things (in this case, حِجَجٍ meaning 'years'). The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) and ى with fatha is added at the end.
Came to mean 'year' because it was the time of pilgrimage
Root letters form a plural by changing vowels inside the word. Originally meant pilgrimage but came to mean 'years' since Hajj happens yearly.
The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows this word is being counted or measured (eight years in this case)
This is the basic conditional particle that shows something depends on something else happening, like saying 'if you study, you'll learn'

steps.m: and if/then if (connects to أَتْمَمْتَ - you complete)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before. Here it connects to the verb أَتْمَمْتَ (you complete), showing what happens after the condition.
Added vowel marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added أ at beginning to show causing something to happen
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' did the action
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to make the basic number word
Added tanween alif to show this is describing additional years being offered (referring to حِجَجٍ/years mentioned earlier)
Basic preposition meaning 'from'. Added kasra and sukun to show proper pronunciation.

steps.m: so from (connects to أَتْمَمْتَ - you completed)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement about completing 8 years. It connects to the word أَتْمَمْتَ (you completed).
Added kasra vowels to show this is in the genitive case after مِنْ
Added second person masculine singular suffix كَ to show possession or relation. This word comes after مِنْ (from) in the ayah.
Basic interrogative/relative pronoun meaning 'what' or 'that which'. In this ayah, it's used with a negative meaning.
Adding وَ at start connects this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
The alif is written long (with آ) to show emphasis in pronunciation. This word negates the verb أُرِيدُ (I want) that comes after it.
Adding 'أ' at the start changes it from 'want' to 'I want' - like adding 'I' in English
The vowel marks ُ and ِ help show this is about 'I' wanting something
This is a connecting word that links two ideas. Here it connects 'I do not want' with 'to burden you'. The fatha and sukun marks help show how to pronounce it correctly.

steps.m: to make something difficult or burdensome

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make the word pronounceable as a past tense verb

steps.m: I make difficult (in أَنْ أَشُقَّ عَلَيْكَ - that I make difficult upon you)
steps.n: Changed شَقَقَ to أَشُقَّ by adding أَ at the start to mean 'I' and changing the middle vowel to ُ to show it's happening now. Used in أَنْ أَشُقَّ عَلَيْكَ meaning 'that I make difficult for you'
Added َ at the end because it comes after أَنْ which needs this ending

steps.m: upon (connected to أَشُقَّ - I make difficult)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows something being imposed on or affecting something else. In this ayah, it connects to the verb أَشُقَّ (I make difficult).
Added يْ as a connecting letter to help pronounce the next suffix smoothly

steps.m: upon you (object of أَشُقَّ - I make difficult)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show this affects 'you' (masculine singular). This word is receiving the action of أَشُقَّ (I make difficult) in this ayah.
These are the three basic letters that give us the core meaning of finding
Added vowels to connect the letters together and make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by: 1) adding تَ at start to show 'you', 2) changing middle vowel to ِ, 3) adding ُ at end, and 4) dropping و because it's weak
Added سَ at the start to show this will happen in the future

steps.m: you will find me (speaking to Allah)
steps.n: Added نِى at the end to show that 'me' is what is being found. The ٓ shows the sound is stretched when reading.
This is a conditional particle that introduces the condition 'if Allah wills' in this ayah. It connects the condition (Allah's will) with what comes before it.
Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he wanted'
The weak letter hamza (ء) combines with alif to make a long vowel
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
This is the unique name for The One True God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ُ shows this is the subject of شَآءَ (willed/wished) in this ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from or is part of something else
Added َ (fatha) at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word الصَّالِحِينَ (the righteous ones). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who has this quality
This forms the regular masculine plural, indicating multiple righteous people
Adding ال makes the word definite
Added fathas to make it past tense. This word introduces what Musa said to the father of the two women.
The middle letter و changes to ا, which is a common change with this type of word
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (dagger alif) to show distance
Adding ل with kasra (ِ) changes 'this' to 'that', showing something farther away

steps.m: that (referring to what was just mentioned)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the end makes it specifically refer to something just mentioned in the conversation. Here it refers to the agreement being discussed.
Adding vowel marks to make the basic noun meaning 'between'

steps.m: between me (connected to وَبَيْنَكَ - 'and between you')
steps.n: Added ى with kasra (ِ) to show this means 'between me'. This word pairs with وَبَيْنَكَ (and between you) in the ayah to show an agreement between two people.
Added vowels to make the word mean 'between' - the space separating things

steps.m: between you (connecting to بَيْنِى - between me)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show this relates to 'you'. This pairs with بَيْنِى (between me) to mean 'between me and you'

steps.m: and between you (connecting to بَيْنِى - between me)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this to بَيْنِى, forming the full phrase 'between me and you'
Root letters combined with fatha and shadda to make a question word meaning 'which'
Adding ما makes the word more general and conditional. In this ayah, it refers to whichever of the two terms (الْأَجَلَيْنِ) that Musa completes.
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make it a noun meaning the time itself

steps.m: two appointed times (object of قَضَيْتُ/I completed)
steps.n: Added يْنِ to show exactly two things. This word is what's being completed in the phrase 'I completed'

steps.m: the two appointed times (object of قَضَيْتُ/I completed)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to point to specific appointed times mentioned in the conversation
Added vowels to make the past tense form. The ي with sukun (يْ) appears because this is a weak root ending in ي.
The تُ at the end shows that 'I' did the action. In this ayah, this is part of Musa's father-in-law speaking to him about completing the agreed term.
Added fatha to show clear pronunciation

Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence of what came before. In this ayah, it's saying 'so there should be no wrongdoing against me' as a result of the agreement mentioned before.
Changed the root letters into a noun by adding the 'u' and 'sukoon' marks to show the act of crossing limits
Added ان at the end makes it mean 'doing something bad in a big way'
Added fatha at the end to show this word receives the action (it's what there is no more of)
This is the basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
Added ّ (shadda) with fatha at the end to show this means 'me'. The shadda represents a doubled ي that got merged together.
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning of divinity but is a unique word.
Added shadda on ل to show it is doubled in pronunciation
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject (doer) of وَكِيلٌ (guardian/trustee) in this ayah

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of وَكِيلٌ)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this to the previous statement, plus alif with hamza wasl (ٱ) which helps pronunciation
Basic preposition showing direction or opposition. Here it connects to the word عُدْوٰنَ (hostility/wrongdoing) to show who the hostility would be against.
Added an alif symbol (ٰ) after the ى to show it's a long vowel sound. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.

steps.m: that which (connects to نَقُولُ - we say)
steps.n: Basic relative pronoun that connects to the verb after it. Here it connects 'upon' (عَلَىٰ) with 'we say' (نَقُولُ) to mean 'upon what we say'
The middle و changes to ا in this common word. This is the basic past tense form.
Changed from past tense 'he said' to present tense 'say'. The middle letter ا changes back to its original و when making this change.
Added نَ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action

steps.m: to take care of or protect something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed the middle vowel to kasra and added ي to show someone who does this action as their role

steps.m: a guardian, protector (witness to what we say)
steps.n: Added tanween damma to show this is describing Allah as a witness or guardian over what is being said
This particle connects this event to what happened before, showing sequence
Added لَ to show purpose or emphasis

steps.m: then when (connecting to قَضَىٰ - he completed)
steps.n: Added مَّا with shadda to show a specific time condition. This connects to the next word قَضَىٰ (he completed), showing when something happened.

steps.m: to decide, judge, complete, or fulfill

steps.n: Added vowel marks to make it 'he decided' in the past tense
Changed final ي to ى with small alif on top because it's at the end of the word
Added superscript alif (ٰ) to indicate the long vowel sound. This word comes after فَلَمَّا (and when) to show when something happened.

steps.m: Moses (the subject of قَضَى - he completed)
steps.n: This is a proper noun (name) that comes from Hebrew 'Moshe'. In Arabic, foreign names often keep their original pronunciation but are written with Arabic letters. The ى at the end shows this is a special foreign name.
Added fathas to connect the letters and make it a noun referring to the time itself rather than the action of setting time
Added ال to make it specific and fatha at the end to show it's the object of قَضَىٰ (he completed/fulfilled)

steps.m: to travel or move from one place to another

steps.n: The root letters س-ي-ر became a past tense verb سَارَ (he traveled). The middle letter ي changed to ا, which is common for hollow verbs.

steps.m: and he traveled (after قَضَىٰ - he completed)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to mean 'and'. This connects this action with the previous action of completing something.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a basic noun
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the preposition بِ
Added هِ at the end to show it belongs to him (Musa)
Added بِ at the beginning to show Musa traveled with his family
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: he noticed/perceived (referring to Moses noticing the fire)
steps.n: Added an alif at the start to mean 'to catch sight of' or 'spot something' - like when you suddenly see something in the distance. This shows Moses spotted the fire from far away.

steps.m: from (showing where the fire is located in relation to جَانِبِ)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it shows that the fire is coming from/located at the side of the mountain.
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb

steps.m: one who goes to the side
steps.n: Changed from a verb (doing) to describing someone who does the action
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to الطُّورِ (Mount Tur) that comes after it
Added damma to create the noun form of the word
Added shadda for emphasis

steps.m: the mountain (location where مُوسَى saw fire)
steps.n: Added 'al' to make it specific and kasra because it follows مِن جَانِبِ (from the side of)
The root letters change to make the simple noun 'fire' by putting a fatha (short 'a' sound) on the first letter and making the middle letter a long 'aa' sound
Added tanween alif to show this is the object that Musa saw (ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا - I perceived a fire)
Added fathas to make basic past tense form
Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ because و changes to ا in hollow verbs
Root letters got vowel marks (َ and ْ) to make the common noun meaning 'family'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something
Added هِ at the end to show the family belongs to him (Musa)
Added لِ (li) at the beginning to mean 'to/for' his family
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed vowels to make it a command for one person
Added helper alif at start to make it easier to pronounce the command
Added وا at the end to make it a command for a group instead of just one person
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'certainly' in English.
Adding ي at the end shows this is about 'me/I'. The ن gets doubled (شدّة) because it's connecting to ي.
The ي is written with a special mark (ى) and an alif (ٓ) on top when it comes at the end of a word. This doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding the long alif at start changes it from 'sense' to 'cause to sense'
Added تُ at the end to show that 'I' did the action
Connected the root letters together. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two vowels, which is common in Arabic words.

steps.m: a fire (object of ءَانَسَ - he perceived)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this is the thing that Musa perceived or noticed. It's like saying 'he saw a fire' where 'a fire' is what's being seen.
This is the basic form of the word that expresses hope or expectation
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic
The word gets a kasra (ِ) at the end to show it will connect to the next word, like adding glue
Adding ىٓ shows this is about 'me' doing something. In this ayah, Musa is expressing hope that he will bring some news or fire from what he saw.
Connected the root letters together
Added hamza with fatha at start and kasra in middle to make it mean 'I bring' (present tense)
Added كُم at the end to show the action is done to multiple people (you all)
This is a preposition that shows something comes from somewhere else
The shadda (ّ) makes the م sound stronger, and the sukun (ْ) shows where the word pauses
Added هَا which refers back to the fire (نَارًا) mentioned earlier in the verse
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic noun form meaning 'news'
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is any news/information, not specific news

steps.m: with some news/information (related to ءَاتِيكُم - I will bring you)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show this news is what Musa will bring to his family. This connects to the verb ءَاتِيكُم (I will bring you) in the ayah.
This is a basic word that gives a choice between two options. Here it connects 'news' (بِخَبَرٍ) with 'burning stick' (جَذْوَةٍ) as two things Moses might bring back.
Root letters combined with vowels to make a noun meaning a piece of burning coal
Added ة to specify one single burning coal
Added tanween kasra because it comes after the preposition مِنَ (from)
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from, like saying 'from' in English
The shadda makes the م stronger to emphasize the meaning
The fatha at the end shows this word connects to the next word النَّارِ (the fire), making it mean 'from the fire'
Connected the root letters and changed the middle و to ا because it's a weak letter. This word means fire, which is related to light.
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific, like saying 'the fire' instead of just any fire. The ن gets a shadda (double mark) after ال.
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah. The alif at the start becomes a special alif (ٱ).
This is a special word (particle) that expresses hope or expectation. The middle letter has a shadda to make it stronger.
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to
Connected root letters with fatha vowels to form basic past tense
Added تَ at the beginning to change from 'he' to 'you' and show it's happening now
Added ط to show doing action to yourself
Added ون to show plural 'you all' form
This is the base particle meaning 'when' that shows something happened after another thing. The shadda (ّ) makes the م sound stronger.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens right after something else, connecting it to what happened before.
The alif at the end is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added fatha marks and changed the final ي to ى because it's at the end of a word. This shows the action happened in the past.
Added alif khanjariya (ٰ) after ى to show it's pronounced as a long 'aa' sound
Added هَا at the end to show the action was directed towards her (referring to the fire Moses saw)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to someone rather than someone doing it
Added و after first letter to help with pronunciation of the ُ sound
Changed the ي to ى because it comes at the end of the word. This word is what happened after أَتَىٰهَا (he came to it).
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows where something starts from or comes from
Added kasra (ِ) under م to show how to pronounce it correctly. In this ayah, it connects 'he was called' (نُودِىَ) with 'the side' (شٰطِئِ) to show where the calling came from.
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'one who/which does the action'
The alif became a long alif and got a kasra ending because it's connected to 'the valley' after it, showing the valley owns/has this bank/shore

steps.m: to flow or move like water

steps.n: Added vowels to make the noun meaning 'valley'. A valley is called this because water flows through it.

steps.m: the valley (the specific valley on the blessed side mentioned in this ayah)
steps.n: Adding ٱلْ makes it refer to the specific valley mentioned in the story of Moses where Allah spoke to him
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic word
Added أ at the start to make it mean 'right side/direction' (like right vs left)
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: of the right (side of الوادِ - the valley)
steps.n: Added kasra at end to show this describes the side of the valley mentioned in the ayah
Starting with the basic letter that forms this preposition
Adding a small line under the letter (kasra) to make it mean 'in'

steps.m: in, within (showing location in ٱلْبُقْعَةِ - the place)
steps.n: Adding the ى at the end completes the preposition. This preposition shows that something is happening within the blessed place mentioned in the ayah.
Added damma and sukun to make it into a noun
Added ة to make it feminine and specify one instance

steps.m: the blessed spot/place (location where Allah spoke to Moses)
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite and ِ at the end because it follows في (in) in the ayah

steps.m: to bless, to increase, to make grow

steps.n: Adding an alif after first root letter makes it about doing something to/with someone else
Shows something receiving blessing rather than giving it
Makes it feminine to match with بُقْعَة (area/place) which is feminine
Shows this word is connected to what comes before it (فِى/in)
Adds ال to specify this particular blessed place where Allah spoke to Moses
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or is part of something
Added a fatha at the end because it comes before the word ٱلشَّجَرَةِ (the tree) which starts with ال. This makes it easier to pronounce.
Connected the root letters with basic vowels
Adding ة at the end makes it mean one tree instead of trees in general
The word gets a different ending (-i sound) because it comes after a word that means 'of/from'

steps.m: of the tree (in the blessed spot)
steps.n: Added the definite article ال which causes the ش to get a shadda

steps.m: that (introduces what was called out to موسى)
steps.n: This particle introduces the content of what was called out to Moses. It connects 'he was called' (نُودِىَ) with what follows.
Added vowel marks to show proper pronunciation of the name Moses. The ىٰ at the end shows this is a special type of alif that appears at the end of some Arabic words.
Added يٰ at the beginning to show we are calling out to Moses. The ٓ at the end shows a slight pause in the sound when calling someone.
Added kasra under إ and shadda on ن to create the emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows

steps.m: indeed I (speaking about myself with emphasis)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to show I am speaking about myself

steps.m: indeed I (speaking about myself with emphasis)
steps.n: Changed the final ي to ى with an alif mark (ٓ) on top - this is just a writing style variation that doesn't change the meaning
This is the base form of the personal pronoun meaning 'I'. Added vowel marks to connect the letters.
Added alif at the end to make it a complete standalone pronoun. In this ayah, Allah uses this emphatic form of 'I' to declare His divine presence to Moses.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific deity rather than any deity
Adding damma shows this word is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: Lord (one who nurtures and cares for all)
steps.n: Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to create a noun meaning 'one who does this action completely'
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject being described by العالمين (of the worlds)

steps.m: to know, understand, or be aware of

steps.n: Forms a noun meaning 'that which is known' - referring to all of creation or existence that can be known
The ين ending makes it plural and shows it's owned by رَبُّ (Lord) in the phrase رَبُّ ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ (Lord of the worlds)
Adding ال makes it specific worlds. The long alif (ٰ) is a spelling variation of ا.
This is a compound particle made up of 'wa' (and) + 'an' (that)
Adding vowels turns the root into a proper past tense verb
Changed to a command by dropping the first vowel, putting sukun on ل, and dropping the final ي. The أَ at start is not added - it's part of the root word.

steps.m: to use a staff or rod

steps.n: The root letters were connected with fathas to make the noun 'staff'. The last letter و changed to ا which is common at the end of Arabic nouns.
Added كَ to show the staff belongs to you. This is what Allah is commanding Moses to throw.
This is a word that shows when something happens. The shadda (double letter) and ا at the end make it specifically mean 'when'.

steps.m: then when (connects to رَءَاهَا - he saw it)
steps.n: The فَ shows this happens right after something else. In this ayah, it connects 'when he saw it' to what happened before.

steps.m: to see with one's eyes or mind

steps.n: Changed the hamza spelling from أ to ء because it comes between two vowels. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: he saw her/it (referring to the staff)
steps.n: Added هَا which means 'her/it' to show what was seen. Here it refers to the staff (عَصَا) mentioned earlier.
Connected the root letters and added shadda on final ز to show doubled letter
Adding ٱ at start and ت after ه changes it from 'shake' to 'shake itself'
Adding ت at start shows this is happening now (present) and the doer is feminine
This is the basic comparison particle that shows similarity
Adding أَنَّ changes the meaning from simple comparison to a more hypothetical comparison

steps.m: as if it/she (referring to عَصَاكَ - your staff)
steps.n: The هَا refers back to the staff (عَصَاكَ) mentioned earlier in the verse, which is feminine in Arabic
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added alif after ج and doubled ن to create intensive active participle

steps.m: a snake (like the staff appeared to be)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is any snake in general, not a specific one. This word completes the comparison in كَأَنَّهَا (as if it were) describing how the staff appeared to move.

steps.m: to turn away or move back

steps.n: The root و-ل-ي became doubled (shadda) to show strong/forceful turning away
Added ى at the end to make it past tense
Added dagger alif above the alif maqsura to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: to turn away or go back

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show who does the action
Added ًا to show this describes how the action of وَلَّىٰ (he turned away) happened - he turned away while turning back
This is a particle that negates verbs and changes their meaning to past tense. It's one of the basic negative words in Arabic that a beginner learns early on.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. In this ayah, it connects to the previous events of Musa seeing the staff move.

steps.m: to follow behind or trace back

steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows doing something multiple times
The يُ at the start changes it from past to present tense, and the ْ at the end shows it's 'not doing' something because of لَمْ before it
Added vowel marks to properly pronounce the name Moses
Added يٰ at the beginning to call out to Moses directly. This is like saying 'Hey Moses!' or 'O Moses!' in English
Added elongation marks (ٰٓ) to show the name is being called out with emphasis
Added sukun on first letter and kasra on middle letter to form basic command pattern
Added hamza with fatha at start and sukun at end to complete command form. This is Allah commanding Moses to come forward after he had turned away in fear from the snake.
This is a negative particle that tells us not to do something. Here it's telling Musa not to fear.
Added و at the beginning to connect this command with what came before it
The root letters خ-و-ف become تَخَاف (you/she fear) by adding تَ at the start and changing و to ا in the middle
The ا is removed and a sukun is added to make it a command form. This word is a negative command because it comes after لا (do not).
إِنَّ is a special word that adds emphasis and certainty to what follows. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'certainly' in English.
Added كَ at the end to show this is talking about 'you' (one male person). This word emphasizes what is being said about you in this ayah.
This is the basic form of the word that shows where something comes from or its source
Added fatha at the end to connect it smoothly to the next word ٱلْءَامِنِينَ (the secure ones). This is like adding glue between words to make them flow better when speaking.
Changes root to show 'someone who' feels safe - like changing 'safety' to 'safe person'
Converting to plural form
Adding ال makes it specific. The word follows مِنَ (from/among) in the ayah, showing these are specific safe people being referred to

steps.m: to insert or thread something through

steps.n: Changes to command form by adding damma and sukun marks
Added hamzat al-wasl (ٱ) at the beginning to help pronunciation since Arabic words can't start with a sukun
The root letters combine to make the simple word for 'hand'. The last letter drops off.
Added َكَ to show this is your hand. This word is what is being inserted (the object of the command 'insert/اسْلُكْ').
This is a basic preposition showing location. Here it shows where to put the hand - in the garment.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: to cut out or make an opening

steps.n: Changed root into a noun meaning the opening at the neck/chest of a garment (bosom) or a pocket
Added ِكَ to show possession ('your'). The kasra (ِ) comes before ك because this word follows the preposition فِى (in/into)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added ت at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense
Changed final vowel to sukun to make it a command
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic word for 'white'
Added اء at the end to make it feminine, commonly used for colors and qualities
Added final vowel mark and lengthened the alif to show it's describing 'your hand' in the verse

steps.m: from (connects غَيْرِ to what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects غَيْرِ (other than) to what comes before it.
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the basic noun form
Added kasra at the end to show this describes the word سُوٓءٍ (harm/evil) that follows it, meaning 'without harm'
Changed from a verb meaning 'to harm' into a noun meaning 'evil' by adding the vowel 'oo' sound (damma)
Added elongation mark (ٓ) over the و to show proper pronunciation
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) to show this describes what kind of evil/harm is being avoided in the phrase 'without any evil/harm'
Added damma on first letter and shadda on final letter to make basic command form
Adds alif at start to make it a stronger command
The و connects this command to the previous one. In context, this refers to pressing one's arms close to their side out of awe.
This is a basic preposition showing direction or movement towards something
The ى changes to ي with sukun when connecting to a pronoun

steps.m: to you (connected to اضْمُمْ - draw in)
steps.n: Added the pronoun كَ (you) to show who is being addressed. This word connects to اضْمُمْ (draw in) in the ayah, showing what to draw in towards.
Added vowels to connect the letters and make it a past tense verb

This form creates a noun from the verb, referring to the thing that inclines (a wing). The word جناح is the direct object of the verb اضمم (meaning 'draw in' or 'fold'), showing what is being drawn in.
The suffix كَ means 'your' (masculine singular). This word is the direct object of the verb ٱضْمُمْ (draw in/fold), showing what is being drawn in.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'from'
The ْ is replaced with َ to connect smoothly to the next word which starts with ال. This is a common change when مِنْ comes before ال.
Changed from basic root letters to a noun meaning 'fear' by adding the vowel marks fatHa and sukun
Added ٱل (the) at the beginning and doubled the ر with shadda
Added ِ at the end because it comes after the word مِنَ (from)
Starting with the basic demonstrative pronoun. The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) called an overbar.
Added نِ to show this refers to two things (the two miracles mentioned: the shining hand and the staff)
Added كَ at the end to show we're pointing to something specific (the two miracles)

steps.m: so these two (referring to the miracles of the shining hand and staff)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this to what came before, showing these miracles are the result of what was mentioned
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters together
Adding ان makes it a strong noun meaning 'proof' instead of just 'proving'
The ان ending shows this is dual (two) in the nominative case
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects 'white' (بَيْضَآءَ) with 'without' (غَيْرِ) to show the whiteness comes without any harm.

steps.m: to care for and help grow

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter makes it stronger - shows complete mastery and care
The kasra at the end shows this Lord belongs to someone
Adding كَ shows this is your Lord. This comes after the word مِن (from) in the ayah
This is the basic preposition showing direction or destination
The alif at the end is written with a small mark (ىٰ) to show it's connecting to the next word فِرْعَوْنَ (Pharaoh). This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic form of the word, borrowed from ancient Egyptian into Arabic as a proper noun title
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the preposition إِلَىٰ (to) in this ayah

steps.m: to gather or come together in a group

steps.n: Adding the basic vowel marks makes it a past tense verb
Changed hamza to alif with kasra due to spelling rules
Added وَ at beginning to mean 'and'
Added ي to show this word is possessed by someone
Added هِ with extension marks to show it belongs to him (Pharaoh)
This is a basic emphasis word that makes statements stronger, like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English
Added هُمْ at the end to show who the word is talking about (they/them)

steps.m: to exist or be in a state

steps.n: Added short vowel marks (fatha) to make it mean 'he existed' in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the past tense
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to create a noun meaning 'people' from the concept of standing together

steps.m: a people (object of كَانُوا - they were)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha (ًا) to show this word is receiving the action of the verb كَانُوا (they were)
Basic vowels added to make it 'he' past tense - means someone disobeyed in the past

steps.m: one who breaks away from obedience
steps.n: Changed to describe someone who does this action

steps.m: those who break away from obedience
steps.n: Made the word plural to show multiple people

steps.m: those who break away from obedience (object)
steps.n: Changed the spelling of alif and added a vowel mark at the end to show this word is receiving the action
Added fathas to make it past tense. This word is what Musa says before addressing his Lord (رَبِّ).
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb
Doubling the middle letter makes this a title for someone who does this action perfectly
Adding kasra at the end makes this a call/address to the Lord
This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger or more certain. The shadda (ّ) shows this strengthening.

steps.m: Indeed I (speaking to رَبِّ - my Lord)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to show that Moses is speaking about himself. This word connects to رَبِّ (my Lord) as Moses is speaking to Allah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past
Changed the last vowel to sukun to prepare for the ending we'll add
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this action. In this ayah, Musa is speaking to his Lord (رَبِّ)
A word that shows something comes from somewhere or someone

steps.m: from them (referring to the people who killed someone from among them)
steps.n: Added هُمْ which means 'them' (for a group of men). In this ayah, Moses is saying he killed someone from among these people, so he fears they will kill him in return.

steps.m: soul/person (acted on by قَتَلْتُ - I killed)
steps.n: Basic word 'n-f-s' got vowels to become 'nafs' meaning a person/soul. Someone did the action of killing to this person.
Added ًا at the end to show this is the object being acted upon (the person who was killed)
Connected the letters and added vowels. The middle و changes to ا because it's a special type of word.
Added أَ at the beginning to show 'I' am doing the action, and ُ at the end to show it's happening now

steps.m: so I fear (because of قَتَلْتُ - I killed)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is happening because of what was mentioned before (that he killed someone)

steps.m: that (connects to يَقْتُلُونِ - they will kill)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that joins two ideas. Here it connects 'I fear' with 'they will kill me'. It's like saying 'I fear that they will kill me' in English.
Basic past tense form showing someone completed the action of killing
Adding the imperfect prefix ي changes this to present/future tense
The ونَ ending shows multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: they kill me (in response to قَتَلْتُ - I killed)
steps.n: The ِ (kasra) at the end shows this is happening to "me". This word appears in Musa's speech where he's expressing fear that they will kill him in response to him having killed one of them (قَتَلْتُ).
The و at the end drops when making the basic noun form. This is common with words ending in و.
Adding ي with kasra (ِ) at the end shows this is 'my' brother
Adding وَ at the start connects this word to what came before. This word refers to Harun (Aaron) who is mentioned right after.
This is a proper noun (name) that doesn't come from a root

steps.m: Aaron (subject of هُوَ أَفْصَحُ - he is more eloquent)
steps.n: The ُ (damma) at the end shows this is the subject of the sentence, where Aaron is being described as more eloquent
This is a personal pronoun meaning 'he'. In this ayah, it refers to Harun (Aaron) who Moses is describing as more eloquent than himself.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added أ at the beginning to make it comparative. This word is comparing Harun's eloquence to Musa's in the ayah.
This is a basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added shadda to strengthen the ن before adding the pronoun
Added ى at the end to show this means 'from me' - the ى means 'me/my' when added at the end of words

steps.m: tongue (specifies how Harun is more eloquent)
steps.n: Root letters ل-س-ن become the noun 'tongue/language' (لِسَان). Shows specifically how Harun is better at speaking than Musa.

steps.m: in tongue/speech (specifies how Harun is more eloquent)
steps.n: Adds -an at the end to show HOW Harun is better - specifically in his speaking ability
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to cause/make someone send'
Changed to a command form and added هُ (meaning 'him') to show who to send

steps.m: so send him! (connected to أَخِى هٰرُونُ)
steps.n: Added فَ to connect this command with the previous statement about Aaron being more eloquent
This is the basic preposition meaning 'with' that shows accompaniment

steps.m: with (ready to connect to a pronoun)
steps.n: The kasra (ِ) is added to prepare the word to connect with a pronoun

steps.m: with me (referring to Moses speaking about himself)
steps.n: Added ي with fatha to show 'me'. In this ayah, Moses is asking Allah to send Aaron with him as a helper who will support his message.
Changes the verb 'to help' into a noun meaning 'helper' - like changing 'help' to 'helper' in English

steps.m: a helper (object of فَأَرْسِلْهُ - so send him)
steps.n: Shows this word is being acted upon by the verb 'send' in the phrase 'so send him as a helper'

steps.m: to tell truth, to be honest

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Adding a shadda (double letter) changes it from 'telling truth' to 'believing strongly in something'
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowel to kasra
Added نِى at the end to show the action is being done to "me"
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly' at the start of a sentence
Added 'I' to show who is speaking. The ن gets doubled and gets a kasra (ِ) sound, and we add a long ي at the end to mean 'I'. In this ayah, Moses is speaking to Aaron.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
The middle weak letter و changes to ا in this pattern
Added أَ at beginning to change from 'he' to 'I', and changed the ending to ُ to show it's happening now. This is like saying 'I am afraid' instead of 'he was afraid'

steps.m: that (connects to يُكَذِّبُونِ - they will deny me)
steps.n: This is a particle that connects 'I fear' (أَخَافُ) to what is feared 'they will deny me' (يُكَذِّبُونِ). It works like 'that' in English when you say 'I fear that they will deny me'

steps.m: to deny, to reject as false

steps.n: Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Added shadda to show intensive meaning
Changed to present tense by adding يُ prefix and adjusting vowels
Added plural masculine ending
Added object pronoun ِ (me) at the end
Basic root ق-و-ل got vowels (َ) added to make it 'he said' in the past tense. This is the person who did the action in سَنَشُدُّ (we will strengthen).
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'we' by replacing يَ with نَ
Added سَ at the beginning to show this will happen in the future

steps.m: to strengthen or support with an arm/helper

steps.n: Changes from root meaning into noun for physical body part (arm)

steps.m: your arm/support (object of سَنَشُدُّ - we will strengthen)
steps.n: Added كَ to show this belongs to 'you' (male). This word is what's being strengthened in the phrase 'We will strengthen your support.'
Added ي to make the word ready for showing possession
The كَ shows this brother belongs to 'you' (male)
The بِ shows this brother is helping or supporting, connecting to عَضُدَكَ (your strength) in the phrase
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
The نَ at the start shows 'we' are doing the action, and the ُ at the end shows it's happening now or in the future

steps.m: and we make/put/establish (for لَكُمَا - for you both)
steps.n: Adds conjunction 'and' to connect with previous statement. This word is connected to لَكُمَا (for you both) which comes after it in the ayah
Added fatha to show the basic pronunciation of the preposition

steps.m: for/to both of you (referring to Moses and Aaron)
steps.n: Added كُمَا which means 'both of you' to specify who the preposition is directed to. In this ayah, Allah is telling Moses that He will give power to both him and his brother Aaron.

steps.m: to dominate or have authority over something

steps.n: Root letters س-ل-ط got vowels (ُ and ْ) to make the noun 'authority'
Added ٰن to intensify the meaning

steps.m: a strong authority/clear proof (as the object being given)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being given in the sentence (We will give you both سُلْطٰنًا)
This is the basic negation word that means 'not' or 'no'

steps.m: so not (negating يَصِلُونَ - they will reach)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this negation is a result of what came before. In this ayah, it's saying that as a result of Allah strengthening Musa and Harun, their enemies will not be able to reach them.

steps.m: to connect or arrive at something

steps.n: Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he connected/arrived' in the past
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. The و drops because it's a weak letter at the start.
Added ونَ to show multiple people are doing the action. In this ayah, they are trying to reach/get to Moses and Aaron.
This is a preposition that shows direction or destination
Added sukun before attaching pronoun
Added كُمَا which means 'both of you' - this shows who the action is directed towards
Root letters combine with a long 'aa' sound (آ) and end with 'ah' (ة) to make a noun meaning 'sign'
Makes the word plural by adding ات
Adds نَا to show these signs belong to us
Adds بِ to show these signs are being used as tools or means. This word connects to يَصِلُونَ (they reach) in the ayah.
This is the basic singular form of the pronoun meaning 'you'
Adding ما at the end changes 'you' from singular to dual (exactly two people)
This is a question word that asks about who does something. Here it's asking about who follows.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added اِ at start and doubled the middle letter to show doing something with extra effort or intensity
Added كُمَا at the end to show the action is done to two people ('you two')
Added كُمَا at the end to show the action was done to 'you two'. This word is the object of يَصِلُونَ (they reach) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who overcomes (doer of the action)
steps.n: Forms active participle meaning 'one who overcomes'. This word is in the nominative case because it is the subject (predicate) of the pronoun أنتما (you two).
Makes word plural to refer to multiple people who overcome
Makes the word definite by adding 'the'
This is the basic form of the word meaning 'when'
Adding ا at the end helps connect this word with what comes after it
Adding فَ at the beginning shows this happens after something else, connecting it to the previous events
Basic past tense verb pattern with fatha marks
Added هُم at the end to show who the action was directed towards (them)

steps.m: Moses (a name meaning 'drawn from water' or 'child of')
steps.n: This is a special name that came from ancient Egyptian or Hebrew into Arabic. It doesn't follow normal Arabic word patterns.

steps.m: Moses (a name meaning 'drawn from water' or 'child of')
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show exactly how to pronounce the name: 'Mūsā'

steps.m: Moses (the one who brought بَيِّنٰتٍ clear signs)
steps.n: Added a shadda (doubling mark) on the م and a small alif at the end to show this is Moses, the prophet who brought miracles from Allah
Forms the basic noun for a single sign or miracle
Changes from one sign/miracle to multiple signs/miracles by adding -aat at the end
Adding preposition بِ meaning 'with'. This word is connected to جَآءَ (came) in the ayah.
Added -i ending because it's connected to another word coming after it
Adding first person plural possessive suffix نَا meaning 'our'. The word is the object of جَآءَ (came) with Musa in the ayah.
Added vowels and doubled middle letter to make it mean 'very/extremely clear'
Added feminine plural ending ات to show multiple clear signs
Changed alif to alif maksura for writing style
Added tanwin kasra to show this word describes 'Our signs' in the ayah
Adding vowel marks to make basic past tense verb

steps.m: they said (referring to those who rejected Moses's signs)
steps.n: The root قَوَلَ changed to قَالُوا by moving the و to make a long aa sound, and adding وا at the end to show many people did the action. This word appears after Moses brought clear signs, showing the response of those who rejected him.
This is a negative particle (حرف نفي) or interrogative particle (حرف استفهام) that has no root since it is a particle (حرف)
Added the fatha mark (َ) to show the correct pronunciation. In this ayah, مَا is being used to say 'this is not magic', showing disagreement with what Musa brought.
Added ه at the beginning to get attention, like saying 'hey, look at this!'
Added an alif mark (ٰ) above the ه to show it's stretched slightly when pronounced
Added fatha (َ) on ذ to show its pronunciation as 'dha'
Changed the final alif to an extended alif (آ) to show it's pronounced longer
This is the base form of the exception particle. It introduces something that is excluded from what was mentioned before.
Added kasra under the first letter to show how to pronounce it correctly

steps.m: except (what comes after is excluded from what was said before)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل and alif at the end to complete the particle. In this ayah, it excludes 'magic' from what Musa brought, showing the disbelievers claimed his signs were nothing but magic.

steps.m: to do magic or cause something to change in a hidden way

steps.n: Changed from an action (doing magic) to a thing (magic itself) by adding the vowels ِ and ْ
Added ٌ to show this is talking about magic in general, not a specific magic
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed from 'he made up' to 'something that was made up', like changing from the action to describing what was invented falsely

steps.m: completely made up, totally invented (describing something false)
steps.n: Added emphasis to show it's completely made up

steps.m: a completely made-up thing (object of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Changed ending to tanween (double mark) to show this is the object - what they called it
This is a negative particle that negates the verb سَمِعْنَا (we heard) in this ayah, meaning 'we did not hear'
The و connects this phrase to what came before it
Connected the root letters together
Added short vowels (fat-ha and kasra) to make it 'he heard' in past tense
Changed final vowel to sukun to prepare for suffix
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action
This is a demonstrative pronoun pointing to something near

steps.m: with/by this (referring to the signs/آيٰت mentioned before)
